items.q: Is external recruitment always better because it brings fresh ideas?
items.correct: Not always — it widens the candidate pool and adds new ideas, but it costs more, takes longer, and the new hire is a bigger unknown than an internal candidate.
items.misconception: Hiring outsiders is automatically superior to promoting existing staff.
items.random_wrong: External recruitment means hiring people from other countries only.
items.why: Balanced comparison of recruitment routes is a standard exam requirement.

items.q: Is training just an avoidable cost?
items.correct: No — training costs time and money, but it raises productivity, quality and motivation, and can cut staff turnover, so it is also an investment.
items.misconception: Cutting training is a cost-free way to save money.
items.random_wrong: Training is only legal for employees under 21.
items.why: Students see the cost line but not the productivity and retention returns.

items.q: Does money motivate everyone in the same way?
items.correct: No — pay matters, but many workers respond more to praise, responsibility, job variety and career prospects; good managers use financial and non-financial methods together.
items.misconception: Higher wages are the only effective motivator.
items.random_wrong: It is illegal to motivate staff without paying bonuses.
items.why: GCSE answers default to pay; non-financial motivation earns the analysis marks.

items.q: Promoting from within is an example.
items.why: Correct answer: Internal recruitment. “External recruitment” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is a SELECTION tool, not a recruitment one?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Interview. “Job advert” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: 60 people apply, 12 are interviewed and 3 are hired. What % of applicants are hired, in %?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
